Items.Title: 来自高级开发人员的 5 个 C# 技巧，可帮助您更好地编程 | 5 C# Tips from a Senior Developer to Help You Program Better
Items.Description:
多年来，高级开发人员倾向于学习许多技巧和解决方法，但他们中的大多数人从未将其写进文章、博客文章或书籍中。 如果您刚开始作为 C# 开发人员，或者如果您希望提高自己的技能，这里有一些高级开发人员关于如何更好地使用 C# 进行编程的技巧。

它们将帮助您使用这种强大的语言提高编码能力，因此，我希望您在继续编程之旅时发现这些技巧对您有用！

这些指导方针由 HTEC Group 的高级软件工程师Milan Jovanović提供， HTEC Group是一家大型科技公司，仅在最新一轮投资中就筹集了高达 1.4亿美元的资金！

我非常推荐你在 Linkedin 上关注他，因为他总是分享有关 C# 的有价值的内容。

表达式体构造函数

对于第一个技巧，Milan 报告说可以使用元组引入的不同特征创建表达式体构造函数：

只要属性或方法仅包含一个表达式，这很有用。

这里米兰首先展示了之前：

 public class Person { public string Name { get ; } public int Age { get ; } public Person ( string name , int age ) { Name = name ; Age = age ; } }

然后，米兰展示了它的外观：

 public class Person { public string Name { get ; } public int Age { get ; } public Person ( string name , int age ) => ( Name , Age ) = ( name , age ); }

有些人乍一看可能会认为这会影响性能，但米兰反驳了这一点：

编译器足够聪明，可以将表达式体赋值优化为单个语句。”

在我看来，这个技巧是一个非常好的实践，尤其是避免多行代码并保持它的清洁和可读性。

米兰首先介绍了这个技巧，我相信你们不止一个人问过自己一个问题：

以前这是不可能的，但随着 .NET 6 的到来，添加了DateOnly和TimeOnly类型。

所以米兰教我们如何将它们付诸实践：

 DateOnly todaysDate = new DateOnly ( 22 , 1 , 20 ); DateOnly currentUtcDate = DateOnly . FromDateTime ( DateTime . UtcNow );
 // A couple constructors to choose from. public TimeOnly ( int hour , int minute ) public TimeOnly ( int hour , int minute , int second ) public TimeOnly ( int hour , int minute , int second , int millisecond ) TimeOnly nineInTheMorning = new TimeOnly ( 9 , 0 ); TimeOnly currentUtcTime = TimeOnly . FromDateTime ( DateTime . UtcNow );

但是，Milan 警告我们，例如PostgreSQL EF Core已经可以毫无问题地处理这些新类型。 另一方面，SQL Server 目前没有：

惰性异常缓存

对于第三个技巧，Milan 向我们介绍了一些关于 .NET Lazy 类的信息。 他想专注于异常缓存行为：

对Lazy.Value属性的后续访问将再次引发该异常。”

此外，他警告说，如果我们不真正理解那部分代码中发生了什么，我们将来可能会遇到不同类型的问题。

 var exceptionTrappingLazy = new Lazy (() => CallExternalService ()); try { // Accessing the Value property will execute CallExternalSe4rvice() // Now, imagine that the external service is down // The call to CallExternalService() throws an exception var lazyValue = exceptionTrappingLazy . Value ; } catch {...} try { //Accesing the Value property again won't call CallExternalService() //It will throw the trapped exception again! var lazyValueAgain = exceptionTrappingLazy . Value ; } catch {...}

最后，米兰提醒我们：

在这 3 个小技巧之后，我们再来看看Milan Jovanović 提供的一系列小技巧。 在接下来的这些技巧中，Milan 解释了如何充分利用 .NET 6 中引入的MinBy和MaxBy方法。

让我们来看看他们吧！

如何使用 MinBy 和 MaxBy LinQ 方法

在这些示例中，米兰使用了几种水果和蔬菜来进行清晰的解释。 首先，了解MinBy和MaxBy工作原理。 为此，我们将使用MinBy找到最小值，使用MaxBy找到最大值。 让我们看看米兰的第一个例子：

 public record Food ( string Name , double Price ); var foodList = new List < Food > { new ( "🍎" , 1.15 ), new ( "🍌" , 1.51 ), new ( "🥥" , 2.99 ), new ( "🍋" , 0.70 ), new ( "🥑" , 9.13 ), new ( "🍄" , 0.05 ), }; //How do me find the cheapest food? // How do we find the most expensive food?

使用 MinBy 找到最小的值对象

首先它展示了它在.NET 5中的样子（没有MinBy ）：

 var cheapest = foodList . OrderBy ( f => f . Price ). List ();

然后，Milan 展示了它在.NET 6中的样子（使用MinBy ）：

 var cheapest = foodList . MinBy ( f => f . Price );

在这种情况下，结果将是具有0.05值的蘑菇“🍄”（在此示例中显然不受通货膨胀的影响）。

对不起，让我们继续米兰的例子：

使用 MaxBy 查找最高值对象

首先它展示了它在.NET 5中的样子（没有MaxBy ）：

 var mostExpensive = foodList . OrderByDescending ( f => f . Price ) . First ();

然后，Milan 展示了它在.NET 6中的样子（使用MaxBy ）：

 var mostExpensive = foodList . MaxBy ( f => f . Price );

在这种情况下，结果将是价值9.13的鳄梨“🥑”（这个受到通货膨胀的影响…… ）。

有了这些最佳实践，即使Milan 发出警告，我们也可以保持更简洁的代码：

目前，它们不存在于 EF 提供程序中。”



原文:

Senior developers tend to pick up many tricks and workarounds over the years, but most of them never make it into articles, blog posts or books. If you’re just starting out as a C# developer, or if you’re looking to improve your skills, here are some tips from some of a Senior Developer on how to program better using C#.

They will help you improve your coding abilities with this powerful language so, I hope you find these tips useful as you continue on your programming journey!

These guidelines are provided by Milan Jovanović, Senior Software Engineer at HTEC Group, a huge technology company that has raised a whopping $140M in its latest round of investment alone!

I very recommend you to follow him on Linkedin because he is always sharing valuable content about C#.

Expression-Bodied Constructors

For this first tip, Milan reports that there is the possibility of creating expression-bodied constructors using the different characteristics introduced by the tuples:

“You can create a tuple from the constructor arguments and then deconstruct that tuple into the respective properties.”

Expression-bodied members have the ability to easily provide the implementation of a member. This is useful as long as the property or method consists of only a single expression.

Here Milan shows first the before:

public class Person
{ public string Name { get; } public int Age { get; } public Person (string name, int age) { Name = name; Age = age; }
}

Then, Milan shows the after of what it would look like:

public class Person
{ public string Name { get; } public int Age { get; } public Person(string name, int age) => (Name, Age) = (name, age);
}

Some people may think at first glance that this affects performance, but Milan disproves this:

“It won’t affect performance! The compiler is smart enough to optimize the expression-bodied assignment into single statements.”

This tip seems to me a very good practice especially to avoid many lines of code and keep it clean and readable.

DateOnly and TimeOnly Types

Milan begins by introducing this tip with a question that I’m sure more than one of you have asked yourself:

“How many times did you only need to know about the Date component of a DateTime object?”

Previously this was not possible, but with the arrival of .NET 6, the Types DateOnly and TimeOnly were added.

And so Milan teaches us how to put them into practice:

DateOnly todaysDate = new DateOnly(22, 1, 20); DateOnly currentUtcDate = DateOnly.FromDateTime(DateTime.UtcNow);

// A couple constructors to choose from.
public TimeOnly(int hour, int minute)
public TimeOnly(int hour, int minute, int second)
public TimeOnly(int hour, int minute, int second, int millisecond) TimeOnly nineInTheMorning = new TimeOnly(9, 0); TimeOnly currentUtcTime = TimeOnly.FromDateTime(DateTime.UtcNow);

However, Milan warns us that, for example PostgreSQL EF Core, already handles these new types without any problem. On the other hand, SQL Server does not at the moment:

“Unfortunately, it seems some EF Core providers — looking at you, SQL Server — still aren’t up to speed with the new types.”

Lazy Exception Caching

For this third tip, Milan tells us a bit about the .NET Lazy class. He wanted to focus on the exception caching behavior:

“If your initialization logic throws an exception, the Lazy instance will cache that exception. Subsequent access to the Lazy.Value property will throw that exception again.”

In addition, he warns that if we don’t really understand what is going on in that part of the code, we may have different kinds of problems in the future.

var exceptionTrappingLazy = new Lazy(() => CallExternalService()); try{ // Accessing the Value property will execute CallExternalSe4rvice() // Now, imagine that the external service is down // The call to CallExternalService() throws an exception var lazyValue = exceptionTrappingLazy.Value;
}
catch {...} try
{ //Accesing the Value property again won't call CallExternalService() //It will throw the trapped exception again! var lazyValueAgain = exceptionTrappingLazy.Value;
}
catch{...}

Finally, Milan reminds us that:

“The lazy instance can and will cache exceptions, and keep throwing them during its lifetime.”

After these 3 tips, let’s look at a series of tips also provided by Milan Jovanović. In these next tips Milan explains how to get the most out of the MinBy and MaxBy methods, which were introduced in .NET 6.

Let’s take a look at them!

How To Use MinBy and MaxBy LinQ Methods

In these examples Milan uses a couple of fruits and vegetables for a clear explanation. First, understand how MinBy and MaxBy work. For this we are going to use MinBy to find the smallest value and MaxBy to find the highest value. Let’s look at Milan’s first example:

public record Food (string Name, double Price); var foodList = new List<Food>
{ new("🍎", 1.15), new("🍌", 1.51), new("🥥", 2.99), new("🍋", 0.70), new("🥑", 9.13), new("🍄", 0.05),
}; //How do me find the cheapest food? // How do we find the most expensive food?

Find the smallest Value Object using MinBy

First it shows how it would look like in .NET 5 (without MinBy):

var cheapest = foodList.OrderBy(f => f.Price).List();

Then, Milan shows how it would look like in .NET 6 (with MinBy):

var cheapest = foodList.MinBy(f => f.Price);

In this case the result would be the mushroom “🍄” with 0.05 value (which apparently is not being affected by inflation in this example).

Sorry, let’s continue with the Milan examples:

Find the highest Value Object using MaxBy

First it shows how it would look like in .NET 5 (without MaxBy):

var mostExpensive = foodList .OrderByDescending(f => f.Price) .First();

Then, Milan shows how it would look like in .NET 6 (with MaxBy):

var mostExpensive = foodList.MaxBy(f => f.Price);

In this case the result would be the avocado “🥑” with 9.13 value (this one has been affected by inflation…).

With these best practices we can maintain a cleaner code even though Milan warns:

“Also, don’t try using them inside an EF Core query!

At the moment, they are not present in the EF provider.”

Items.Title: Monorepos，或 Escape 跨时代的沉淀 | Monorepos, or the sedimentation of Escape across ages
Items.Description:
这篇文章摘自Escape 博客上的一篇原创文章

本文是两部分系列中的第一篇。 它将讨论有关 monorepo 及其在 Escape 中的使用的理论指导方针和目标。 第二篇文章将重点介绍这些目标的具体实现。

介绍

我们决定遵循所谓的 monorepo 工作流程。 这个存储库是我们的基础； 它的层次是我们（简明）的历史。

本文描述了我们是如何构建这个存储库的，并回顾了它是如何随着 Escape 作为一家公司的规模而发展的。 以下是您将在本文中找到的内容列表：

  • 我们做出的历史决定最终都是错误的，我们为什么做出这些决定，以及随着时间的推移它们如何影响我们。
  • 现在团队正在成长，用于构建我们的 monorepo 的指南。

过去的好时光

最初，团队很小，由不到五名开发人员组成，他们都负责自己的堆栈部分。 无论我们尝试启动本地环境、编码、提交、拥有绿色 CI 还是部署，我们的整个开发工作流程都发生在一个地方。

单一安装，启动本地环境的单一命令，是唯一定义的。 因此，我们可以依靠技术团队中的各种专业知识。

最后，借助这个 monorepo，我们拥有一个小型技术团队，成功地维护了一个成熟的企业级技术工作流程，包括：

  • 多项服务
  • 持续集成和部署
  • 使用单个命令行安装和启动完整的本地环境

话虽如此，在这一点上，我们的 monorepo 存在的主要原因是方便。 它并没有影响我们编写代码的方式，而且这个 monorepo 拥有几个单体项目。

这里的 Monolithic 没有提到微服务/单体架构的争论，而是在 monorepo 中没有共享依赖项这一事实。 每个项目都是一个诗歌或 npm 项目。 简而言之，我们的根存储库管理适用于不涉及软件相关代码的所有内容。

几个月后，我们不再是三个开发人员，而是 5 个，然后将近 10 个。每个项目中的代码都变得越来越大。 几个月前，代码库已经到了在这个 monorepo 中开发起来很痛苦的地步。

就代码量和复杂性而言，这些项目变得过于繁重，使一切变得更加困难，并带来以下后果：

  • 更长的安装 在能够单独编码之前安装太多的依赖项。
  • 过于复杂 新开发人员在现有代码库中导航并因此入职是一项挑战。 维护者更难确保非破坏性更改。 在生产中发布新功能需要更长的时间（在开发过程中获得反馈的时间更长，发布的时间也更长）
  • 构建 CI 过长
  • 当我们需要更高效的工作流程和架构并围绕这些进行培训时，我们（整个技术团队）倾向于认为我们需要的开发人员比需要的更多。 当然，这是自以为是的，更不符合事实。

解决 Escape 的复杂性

所以，现在，我们的 monorepo 持有的项目的复杂性正式成为一个问题。 我们如何解决它？ 我们需要在 monorepo 的理念上更进一步（或许多）。

较小的项目意味着许多但较小且并行化的构建，更多但更短的 CI（要安装的依赖项更少，每个项目的 lint/测试代码更少）。 这也意味着小部分代码的更多可重用性/可测试性，不可避免地导致更轻的单元测试。

下一部分描述了我们现在针对在 monorepo 中开发的工作流程，我们尝试利用其在标准化方面的优势并使用它来编写较小的项目。

对 monorepos 的第一个兴趣来自对标准化的需求。 如果满足以下两个条件，一个好的 monorepo 有助于降低复杂性：

在下一节中，easy 表示“使用单个记录命令完成”。

  1. 很容易搭建一个满足公司所有要求的新子项目。
  2. 作为共享依赖系统的一部分，很容易将项目链接在一起。

必须了解单一回购是公司范围内的决策。 它涉及技术行为的后果以及团队的组织方式。

标准化项目结构

满足要求 1) 要求 Escape 可以在 monorepo 中快速启动一个新项目。 此外，每个项目一经创建，就应符合这些公司范围内的标准：

  • 符合标准的构建配置（在 monorepo 中使用唯一的每种语言，最终扩展配置）
  • 标准化 IDE 配置
  • 如果您打算使用 Git 挂钩配置（我们这样做！请参阅我们宣布 Mookme 的文章）
  • 使用适当的配置、标准化的覆盖评估进行开箱即用测试

上面的所有内容都应该在使用单个命令创建的项目中搭建。 开始一个成熟的新项目需要 30 秒的时间。

依赖管理

此外，确保 2) 将意味着以下内容：

  • 一条命令就足以声明项目 A 依赖于项目 B
  • 该声明应该足以将项目 B 包含在项目 A 的构建中（以及其他依赖项，并且只有这些依赖项）
  • 这个声明应该足以在 B 更新时触发 A 的质量控制
  • 当开发项目 A 需要对项目 B 进行更改时，我只需要在项目 B 上启动一个 watcher 来触发项目 A 上的 watcher 进行新的更改。

结论

回顾我们的存储库以前的结构，我们更容易定义我们希望 Escape 的技术结构如何。 这使我们制定了一套适合我们公司情况的规则。 感谢您在 Escape 工程的短暂历史上的这次小远足中与我相处！

本系列的下一部分将关注围绕该标准的具体实施所面临的实际挑战。

在 Twitter 上关注 Escape ，或订阅我们的时事通讯以获得更多人类可读的开发人员体验和应用程序安全内容！



原文:

This post is taken from an original article on Escape's blog

This article is the first of a two-part series. It will discuss theoretical guidelines and goals regarding the monorepo and its usages at Escape. The second article will focus on the concrete realization of these goals.

Introduction

Every team at Escape works in the same repository. We decided to follow the so-called monorepo workflow. This repository is our ground; its layers are our (concise) history.

This article describes how we structured this repository and gives a look back at how it evolved alongside the size of Escape as a company. Here is a list of things you will find in this text:

  • Historical decisions we made that ended up being mistakes, why we made them, and how they impacted us over time.
  • Guidelines employed to structure our monorepo now that the team is growing up.

The good old days

Initially, the team was tiny and made of less than five developers, all responsible for their own part of the stack. The monorepo was there primarily to make the management more effortless. Our entire development workflow happens in one place, whether we try to start the local environment, code, commit, have a green CI, or deploy.

Single install, a single command to start the local environment, is uniquely defined. Thus, it becomes possible for us to rely on the diverse expertise within the technical team.

In the end, with a small technical team, thanks to this monorepo, we managed to maintain a fully-fledged enterprise-grade technical workflow involving:

  • multiple services
  • continuous-integration and deployments
  • complete local environments installed and started with a single command line

All that being said, at this point, our monorepo was there for the main reason of convenience. It did not impact how we write code, and this monorepo was holding several monolithic projects.

Monolithic here does not reference the microservice/monolithic architecture debate but rather the fact that there were no shared dependencies within the monorepo. Each project was a poetry or npm project. In a nutshell, our root-repository management was for everything not involving software-related code.

️Days went by, months, and Escape kept growing. A few months later, we were not three developers anymore, but 5, then almost 10. The code within every project has gone bigger and bigger. A few months ago, the codebase reached a point where it is painful to develop in this monorepo.

The projects have become too heavy, in terms of the amount of code, of complexity, making everything harder, with the following consequences:

  • Longer installations Too many dependencies to install before being able to code on its part.
  • Too much complexity It is challenging for a new developer to navigate the existing codebase and thus to be onboarded. It is harder for the maintainers to ensure non-breaking changes. It takes longer to ship new features in production (more time to get feedback during development and more time to ship it)
  • Longer builds CIs too long
  • We (the technical team as a whole) are tempted to think that we need more developers than needed when we would need more efficient workflows and architectures and to be trained around these. This is opinionated and much less factual, of course.

Solving the complexity of Escape

So, now, the complexity of the projects held by our monorepo is officially a concern. How do we solve it? We need to go a (or many) steps further in the philosophy of the monorepo.

Monorepos are supposed to hold many small projects, not a small number of significant projects.

Smaller projects mean many but small & parallelized builds, more but shorter CIs (fewer dependencies to install, less code to lint/test per project). It also means more reusability/testability of small portions of the code, inevitably leading to lighter unit tests.

This next part describes our now-targeted workflow for developing in a monorepo, where we try to leverage its advantages in terms of standardization and use it to write smaller projects.

The thesis of Escape's monorepo

The first interest in monorepos comes from the need for standardization. A good monorepo helps break down complexity if it meets these two conditions:

In the following section, easy means "accomplished with a single documented command."

  1. It is easy to scaffold a new sub-project meeting all the company's requirements.
  2. It is easy to link projects together as part of a shared-dependency system.

It is essential to understand that a monorepo is a company-wide decision. It involves consequences for the technical acts and how the team is organized.

Standardized project structure

Living up to requirement 1) requires that it is possible at Escape to start a new project in the monorepo quickly. Also, every project, as soon as it is created, should conform to these company-wide standards:

  • Up-to-the-standard build configuration (using a unique-per-language across the monorepo, eventually-extended configuration)
  • Same for linting and formatting (using unique-per-language, eventually extended configurations)
  • Standardized IDE configurations
  • Git hooks configurations if you intend to use them (we do ! See our article announcing Mookme)
  • Testing out of the box with proper configurations, standardized coverage assessments

Everything above should be scaffolded in the project created with a single command. Starting a fully-fledged new project needs to be a 30-seconds business.

Dependencies management

Besides, ensuring 2) would mean the following:

  • A single command is enough to declare that project A is dependent on project B
  • This declaration should be enough to include project B in the build of project A (alongside other dependencies and only these dependencies)
  • This declaration should be enough to trigger A's quality controls when B is updated
  • When developing project A with something needing changes on project B, I only need to start a watcher on project B to trigger the watcher on project A for new changes.

Conclusion

Looking back at how our repository was formerly structured, it is easier for us to define how we want the technical of Escape to be structured. This led us to a set of rules that fit our situation as a company. Thanks for bearing with me during this small hike across the short history of Escape's engineering!

The next part of this series will focus on the practical challenges leaning around the concrete enforcement of this standard.

Follow Escape on Twitter, or subscribe to our newsletter for more human-readable developer experience & application security content!
